loops.title: Ticket to verified fix
loops.summary: Turn a bug report or small defect into the smallest patch with proof.
bestFor: A concrete behavior is wrong or failing.
The expected behavior is known.
A focused test or reproduction can prove the fix.
avoidWhen: The product decision is still unresolved.
The requested change is a broad refactor without a failing behavior.
loops.defaultObjective: Fix the reported behavior without unrelated refactors.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the original reproduction and the narrowest relevant regression check.
stopConditions: The reproduction and regression check pass.
The issue cannot be reproduced after two serious attempts.
The next step requires product clarification or access approval.
questions: What exact behavior is wrong?
What command, test, or click path proves the bug?
What changed most recently around this area?
loops.body: Reproduce the issue, identify the smallest root cause, patch it, add or update regression coverage when practical, rerun the reproduction and relevant checks, then decide whether another pass is justified.
goodExamples: make the repro pass with a focused patch
badExamples: broad refactor with no failing behavior

commonMisroutes.reason: CI failure repair starts from job output, not a product bug report.
Coverage work starts from an uncovered behavior, not a reported defect.

reliability.summary: Reliable as a starter when the wrong behavior and a focused reproduction are available.
bestWhen: A concrete wrong behavior and expected behavior are known.
A reproduction or focused regression check can be run.
The desired fix should stay small and behavior-preserving.
failsWhen: The issue cannot be reproduced or observed.
The request is broad feature work disguised as a bug.
No check can prove the behavior changed.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the original reproduction plus the narrowest regression check covering the behavior.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to turn a specific bug report into a small fix with proof.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when one behavior is wrong, the expected behavior is known, and you can reproduce it.
userGuide.starterRequest: Fix the broken checkout redirect and prove the reproduction now passes.
userGuide.firstStep: Paste the bug report, expected behavior, and the reproduction or failing test.
userGuide.proofTip: Keep the proof tied to the original behavior, then add the narrowest regression check you can run.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it for vague product changes, broad refactors, or red CI logs without a product bug.

loops.title: Failing CI repair
loops.summary: Repair a failing build, lint, type-check, or test job with the smallest verified change.
bestFor: A specific CI job or local command is failing.
Logs or failure output are available.
The goal is to get checks green without broad cleanup.
avoidWhen: There is no failure output or command to inspect.
The failure depends on missing secrets or production-only access.
loops.defaultObjective: Fix the failing check with the smallest safe change.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the failing command locally or the closest available equivalent.
stopConditions: The failing check passes.
The same failure repeats twice without new evidence.
The check requires unavailable secrets, services, or approval.
questions: Which CI job or command is failing?
What is the most relevant failure output?
Did the failure start after a specific commit or dependency change?
loops.body: Read the failing output first, reproduce locally when practical, isolate the smallest failing surface, patch the root cause, rerun the failing command, then broaden verification only after the narrow check passes.
requiredSignals: specific failing command or CI job
goodExamples: make lint pass after dependency update
badExamples: fresh install fails for a new user
security risk with no CI failure
exampleChecks: Broaden to npm run check after the narrow check passes

commonMisroutes.reason: First-run setup failures need a clean-environment loop.
Publishing needs release verification and approval gates.

reliability.summary: Reliable as a starter for a single failing command, CI job, or test with usable output.
bestWhen: The failing command, job, or test name is known.
Failure output is available before edits begin.
The goal is to get the same check green with minimal change.
failsWhen: The failure is flaky and cannot be reproduced or isolated.
Several unrelated jobs are failing at once.
The fix requires product or infrastructure approval before code changes.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the failing command locally or the closest available equivalent until it passes.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to repair one failing CI, lint, build, type-check, or test job.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when you have failing command output and want the smallest change that makes it pass.
userGuide.starterRequest: Fix the failing CI job using the log output and rerun the failing command.
userGuide.firstStep: Paste the failing command, the most relevant error lines, and any recent related changes.
userGuide.proofTip: Rerun the exact failing command first; broaden checks only after the original failure is gone.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it for a user-facing bug report unless the CI output is the main evidence.

loops.title: Documentation sweep
loops.summary: Find and fix stale setup, API, command, or workflow documentation.
bestFor: Docs may not match the current implementation.
Install, usage, or release steps need verification.
The work can be scoped to a small doc surface.
avoidWhen: The implementation itself is still changing rapidly.
The requested output is marketing copy with no implementation reference.
loops.defaultObjective: Update stale documentation for the current codebase.
loops.defaultCheck: Verify changed commands, links, examples, and referenced file paths.
stopConditions: No material mismatch remains in the selected scope.
Verification is blocked by missing access or external services.
The iteration budget is reached.
questions: Which docs or workflow feel stale?
What command or behavior should the docs match?
Is this for users, maintainers, or reviewers?
loops.body: Compare the selected docs to the actual code and package behavior, update the highest-impact mismatch, verify examples or links where practical, then rescan for the next mismatch.
requiredSignals: implementation or command to compare against
exampleChecks: Compare docs against current CLI help

commonMisroutes.reason: Clean setup validates the full first-run path before docs-only cleanup.
Release work includes docs plus package and install proof.

reliability.summary: Reliable as a starter when docs can be compared against commands, files, or implementation behavior.
bestWhen: A specific documentation surface is in scope.
Commands, links, examples, or file paths can be verified.
The intended reader or setup path is clear.
failsWhen: There is no implementation or command source of truth.
The docs require policy, marketing, or positioning decisions.
External services block verification.
reliability.requiredCheck: Verify changed commands, links, examples, referenced paths, and any documented setup path.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to make stale docs match how the project actually works now.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when install steps, commands, examples, or screenshots may be out of date.
userGuide.starterRequest: Audit the setup docs against the current package commands and update stale instructions.
userGuide.firstStep: Name the docs path and the source of truth, such as package scripts, CLI output, or working code.
userGuide.proofTip: Run or manually verify the documented command path instead of only proofreading text.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it for open-ended content strategy or documentation that has no implementation source of truth.

loops.title: Review repair
loops.summary: Address blocking review findings until the diff is ready to ship.
bestFor: There is a diff, PR, or reviewer feedback to address.
Findings can be ordered by severity.
Relevant checks can be rerun after each repair.
avoidWhen: There is no concrete diff or finding.
The requested change is a redesign rather than repair.
loops.defaultObjective: Fix blocking review findings and prove the diff is ready for review.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the narrowest checks covering the repaired findings.
stopConditions: Only accepted low-risk findings remain.
Verification is blocked.
A finding requires product or security approval.
questions: Where is the diff or reviewer feedback?
Which finding blocks shipping?
What check should prove the repair?
loops.body: Review the diff for correctness, tests, security, UX, and maintainability risks; fix the highest-severity actionable finding; rerun relevant checks; repeat until no blocking findings remain.
goodExamples: fix reviewer finding and rerun checks
repair code review comments before merge
exampleChecks: Run the tests covering the repaired finding
Confirm only accepted low-risk findings remain

commonMisroutes.reason: Scenario evaluation starts with user flows rather than review comments.
Security findings need explicit trust-boundary and approval gates.

reliability.summary: Useful but still human-dependent because review severity and acceptance criteria vary by team.
bestWhen: There is an existing diff, PR, or review note.
Blocking findings are concrete and actionable.
Relevant checks can cover each repaired finding.
failsWhen: Review comments are subjective or contradictory.
The requested repair expands into product redesign.
No reviewer or check can confirm the fix.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the narrowest checks covering the repaired findings and record any accepted residual risk.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to resolve concrete review comments without widening the diff.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when a reviewer gave actionable findings and you need a clean follow-up pass.
userGuide.starterRequest: Address the blocking review comments and rerun the checks tied to the changed files.
userGuide.firstStep: Paste the review findings, current diff scope, and the check reviewers expect to pass.
userGuide.proofTip: Close each blocking comment with a targeted change and rerun the nearest relevant check.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it before there are real review findings or when the next step is a product decision.

loops.title: Release readiness
loops.summary: Prepare a package, app, or feature for a public release with evidence.
bestFor: The feature is implemented and needs final packaging.
Release notes, versioning, and install checks matter.
The user needs a clear go/no-go answer.
avoidWhen: Core implementation is still incomplete.
Publishing requires credentials or external approval not yet granted.
loops.defaultObjective: Prepare the current change for release and prove the public install or usage path works.
loops.defaultCheck: Run package checks, dry-run publish or build, and a fresh install or production smoke.
stopConditions: Release verification passes.
Publishing needs explicit approval.
A blocking package, build, or deployment failure remains.
questions: What artifact is being released?
What install or usage path must work for a new user?
Do you want publish/deploy actions performed or only prepared?
loops.body: Check versioning and public docs, run the package or app verification gate, dry-run the release path, perform approved publishing steps, then verify from a fresh environment or production URL.
exampleChecks: Run npm pack --dry-run or build dry-run
Verify from a fresh install or public URL

commonMisroutes.reason: Dependency compatibility should be proven before release readiness.
A red check needs repair before release verification.

reliability.summary: Reliable as a starter for package or app releases when the publish/build path can be dry-run and smoked.
bestWhen: Versioning, changelog, and publish target are known.
Package checks, build checks, or dry-run commands exist.
A fresh install or production smoke can verify the public path.
failsWhen: Publishing requires credentials, OTP, or approval that is not available.
Release criteria are not defined.
The target registry or deployment system is unavailable.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run package checks, dry-run publish or build, and a fresh install or production smoke where practical.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this before publishing or shipping so the public install path is verified.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when the code is nearly ready and the risk is packaging, versioning, or release drift.
userGuide.starterRequest: Prepare the npm package release and prove the packed artifact contains the right files.
userGuide.firstStep: Name the target version, release command, package manager, and required prepublish checks.
userGuide.proofTip: Run the same check that publish or deploy will run, plus a dry-run pack or install path when possible.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it while core implementation work is still unclear or failing unrelated tests.

loops.title: Fresh setup
loops.summary: Validate a clean checkout or clean project setup and repair hidden assumptions.
bestFor: A new user or agent must install and run the project.
Setup docs may hide missing prerequisites.
The work should expose first-run failures.
avoidWhen: The environment requires private credentials that cannot be mocked.
The goal is feature work unrelated to setup.
loops.defaultObjective: Prove a fresh project setup works from the documented instructions.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the documented install and first verification command from a clean directory.
stopConditions: Fresh setup succeeds.
A required private dependency or credential blocks verification.
The same setup failure repeats after a documented repair.
questions: Which setup path should a new user follow?
What command proves setup is complete?
Can private services be mocked or skipped?
loops.body: Start from a clean directory, follow the documented setup, record the first failure, fix the smallest missing assumption in docs or code, then retry cleanly.
goodExamples: prove npx install works in a temp project
exampleChecks: Run setup from a clean directory
Run the documented first verification command
Retry after fixing the smallest missing assumption

commonMisroutes.reason: Publishing includes fresh install proof plus release approval gates.
Docs-only drift does not require a clean setup loop.

reliability.summary: Reliable as a starter for finding hidden setup assumptions from a clean environment.
bestWhen: Setup instructions already exist.
A clean directory or environment can be used.
The first verification command is documented.
failsWhen: Private credentials or dependencies are required but unavailable.
The setup path depends on undocumented manual account state.
The goal is broad onboarding redesign rather than setup verification.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the documented install path and first verification command from a clean directory.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to prove a new user can follow the setup instructions from scratch.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when install, onboarding, or local setup might rely on hidden machine state.
userGuide.starterRequest: Follow the README setup in a clean directory and fix any missing assumptions.
userGuide.firstStep: Create or describe a clean environment, then follow the public docs exactly as written.
userGuide.proofTip: Retry from a fresh state after edits so you know the setup path works without local leftovers.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it for one failing test inside an already configured development environment.

loops.title: Test coverage gap
loops.summary: Add focused tests around risky behavior without broad test churn.
bestFor: A behavior is important but poorly covered.
A recent fix needs regression coverage.
The target test layer is known.
avoidWhen: The desired behavior is not defined.
The test suite is too unstable to trust.
loops.defaultObjective: Add focused coverage for the risky behavior without unrelated rewrites.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the new or changed tests and the narrowest related suite.
stopConditions: The target behavior is covered and checks pass.
The behavior needs product clarification.
The same test infrastructure failure repeats.
approvalGates: changing product behavior only to satisfy tests
questions: Which behavior is under-tested?
Which test layer should cover it?
What failure would the test catch?
loops.body: Identify the smallest behavior contract, add a focused test that would fail without the intended behavior, run the narrow test, then broaden only if the change touches shared behavior.
goodExamples: add regression coverage for checkout edge case
badExamples: fix failing CI without a coverage gap
exampleChecks: Confirm the test would fail without the behavior

commonMisroutes.reason: Repair the failing check first unless the missing test is the blocker.
Scenario evaluation is broader than adding focused regression tests.

reliability.summary: Reliable as a starter when the risky behavior can be described as a small testable contract.
bestWhen: A specific behavior lacks coverage.
A focused test can fail before the intended fix.
The surrounding test framework is already available.
failsWhen: The behavior still needs product clarification.
The test requires brittle snapshots or broad end-to-end setup.
The change would require rewriting the test architecture.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the new or changed tests plus the narrowest related suite.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to add focused tests for behavior that is real but under-protected.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when the code may work but an important edge case lacks regression coverage.
userGuide.starterRequest: Add regression coverage for the checkout edge case and run the focused test command.
userGuide.firstStep: Name the behavior, existing test area, and the smallest case that should fail without coverage.
userGuide.proofTip: Prove the test is meaningful by targeting the behavior, not just increasing coverage numbers.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it for code cleanup, performance work, or bugs with no clear expected behavior.

loops.title: UX polish
loops.summary: Improve a specific user flow for clarity, responsiveness, and accessibility.
bestFor: The screen exists but users may not understand what to do.
A specific page or flow needs clearer hierarchy.
Responsiveness or accessibility can be checked.
avoidWhen: The product goal is not defined.
The request is purely decorative without a user action.
loops.defaultObjective: Make the selected flow easier to understand and complete without adding unnecessary scope.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the relevant UI smoke test or manually verify desktop and mobile states.
stopConditions: The primary user action is clear and verified.
Further changes are subjective without user feedback.
The next improvement requires a product decision.
questions: What is the one user action this flow must make easier?
Which screen or viewport is confusing?
How should we verify the improvement?
loops.body: Identify the primary user action, remove or rewrite confusing UI, verify key states and responsive behavior, then stop when the flow is understandable without extra explanation.
goodExamples: make the generated prompt easier to find
improve mobile clarity for a form flow
reduce confusing copy around a CTA
exampleChecks: Manually verify desktop and mobile states
Check keyboard focus and visible next action
Run the relevant UI smoke test

commonMisroutes.reason: Scenario pass/fail work is broader than UI polish.
Speed work needs baseline and remeasurement.

reliability.summary: Useful but subjective unless the primary user action and verification path are explicit.
bestWhen: One user flow and one primary action are in scope.
Confusing copy, layout, states, or responsiveness can be observed.
Desktop and mobile checks can be performed.
failsWhen: The request is open-ended aesthetic preference without a user task.
The page needs product positioning decisions first.
No manual or automated UI verification path exists.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the relevant UI smoke test or manually verify the primary flow across desktop and mobile states.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to make one user flow clearer, more usable, and easier to trust.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when users can complete the task but the screen is confusing, cramped, or unclear.
userGuide.starterRequest: Make the onboarding flow easier to understand on mobile without changing the core feature.
userGuide.firstStep: Name the screen, target user, primary action, and the confusion you want removed.
userGuide.proofTip: Check desktop and mobile screenshots for hierarchy, spacing, empty states, and text fit.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it when the underlying product decision or data model still needs to be designed.

loops.title: Performance measurement
loops.summary: Improve speed, memory, bundle size, or latency using before-and-after evidence.
bestFor: There is a measurable target or known slow path.
A baseline can be captured before changes.
Correctness checks can run after optimization.
avoidWhen: No reliable measurement exists.
The proposed change sacrifices correctness or UX without proof.
loops.defaultObjective: Improve the measured bottleneck while preserving behavior.
loops.defaultCheck: Record a baseline, rerun the same measurement, and run the relevant correctness check.
stopConditions: The target threshold is met.
Measurement is unreliable.
Changes stop producing meaningful improvement.
questions: What metric should improve?
How do we measure the baseline?
What correctness check must still pass?
loops.body: Capture a baseline, make one focused optimization, remeasure under the same conditions, keep only proven wins, then target the next bottleneck if another pass is justified.
goodExamples: reduce bundle size with before and after proof
improve API latency without changing behavior

commonMisroutes.reason: Structural cleanup needs behavior-preserving checks more than performance metrics.
Clarity and responsiveness are UX polish unless speed is measured.

reliability.summary: Reliable as a starter only when baseline and comparison measurements are repeatable.
bestWhen: A specific bottleneck or metric is named.
A baseline can be captured before edits.
The same measurement can be rerun after each change.
failsWhen: The measurement is noisy or not reproducible.
The desired improvement threshold is undefined.
Correctness checks are missing after optimization.
reliability.requiredCheck: Record a baseline, rerun the same measurement, and run the relevant correctness check.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to improve speed only after measuring what is slow.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when you can capture a baseline and compare the same metric after one focused change.
userGuide.starterRequest: Measure the slow dashboard load, make one focused improvement, and compare before/after results.
userGuide.firstStep: Record the baseline command, page, metric, input size, and environment before editing.
userGuide.proofTip: Keep only changes that improve the measured metric without breaking functional checks.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it for speculative optimization or broad rewrites without a repeatable measurement.

loops.title: Dependency upgrade
loops.summary: Upgrade one dependency or toolchain surface with compatibility proof.
bestFor: One dependency or small dependency group needs upgrading.
Release notes or migration notes are known.
Compatibility can be verified with existing checks.
avoidWhen: The upgrade spans many unrelated packages.
The package is security-sensitive and lacks a rollback plan.
loops.defaultObjective: Upgrade the selected dependency with the smallest compatibility patch.
loops.defaultCheck: Run install, type-check, tests, and the narrow feature smoke covering the dependency.
stopConditions: The upgrade passes compatibility checks.
A breaking change requires design approval.
The dependency introduces unacceptable risk.
questions: Which dependency should be upgraded?
Is this a patch, minor, or major upgrade?
What feature or check proves compatibility?
loops.body: Upgrade the smallest dependency surface, inspect migration notes when needed, repair compatibility issues, run focused checks, and stop before broad lockfile churn.
goodExamples: update one npm package with tests
apply migration notes for a toolchain update
exampleChecks: Run the narrow feature smoke using the dependency

commonMisroutes.reason: Publishing current code is release readiness, not dependency upgrade.
Security-sensitive upgrades need risk and approval handling.

reliability.summary: Reliable as a starter for scoped dependency upgrades with focused compatibility checks.
bestWhen: The dependency and desired version range are known.
Migration notes or release notes can be inspected when needed.
Install, type-check, tests, or feature smoke checks exist.
failsWhen: The upgrade causes broad lockfile churn outside the selected surface.
Breaking changes require design or product approval.
The dependency is security-sensitive and needs policy review first.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run install, type-check, tests, and the narrow feature smoke covering the dependency.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to upgrade one dependency while containing compatibility risk.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when a package needs a version bump and you can run focused compatibility checks.
userGuide.starterRequest: Upgrade React and fix only the compatibility issues needed for tests to pass.
userGuide.firstStep: Name the package, target version, lockfile state, migration notes, and affected checks.
userGuide.proofTip: Run the package manager check, type/build check, and focused behavior tests after the bump.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it for sweeping dependency refreshes where many packages change for unrelated reasons.

loops.title: Security hardening
loops.summary: Reduce a concrete security risk with scoped evidence and approval gates.
bestFor: A specific security concern or finding exists.
The affected boundary can be scoped.
A test, review, or config check can prove the fix.
avoidWhen: The request is a broad audit with no target.
The fix would require secret rotation or production changes without approval.
loops.defaultObjective: Fix the concrete security risk without unrelated refactors.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the relevant security, auth, validation, or regression check.
stopConditions: The security finding is addressed with evidence.
The next step requires credentials, production access, or policy approval.
The risk cannot be reproduced from available context.
questions: What specific risk or finding should be fixed?
Where is the trust boundary?
What check or review proves the risk is reduced?
loops.body: Scope the trust boundary, reproduce or reason about the risk, patch the smallest risky path, add validation or regression coverage when practical, and document residual risk.
goodExamples: remove exposed secret from config path
exampleChecks: Run relevant auth or validation tests

commonMisroutes.reason: General review comments use review repair unless the finding is security-specific.
A dependency-only compatibility patch may fit dependency upgrade with security gates.

reliability.summary: Reliable as a starter when the trust boundary and concrete risk are known.
bestWhen: A specific input, auth, permission, data exposure, or dependency risk is identified.
The risky path can be patched without unrelated refactors.
A security, validation, auth, or regression check can cover the change.
failsWhen: The risk is only a vague concern without an attack path.
The fix needs credentials, production access, or policy approval.
The mitigation would change product behavior without approval.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the relevant security, auth, validation, or regression check and document residual risk.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to reduce one concrete security risk with a focused fix and proof.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when there is a specific unsafe input, auth boundary, secret, or exposure to harden.
userGuide.starterRequest: Sanitize the unsafe user input before saving it and add a regression check.
userGuide.firstStep: Name the asset, attacker-controlled input, trust boundary, and required security behavior.
userGuide.proofTip: Run the exploit-oriented regression check plus the nearest normal behavior check.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it for broad security audits with no specific finding or threat scenario.

loops.title: Refactor containment
loops.summary: Refactor a narrow area while proving behavior stays the same.
bestFor: The behavior should stay the same.
The scope can be limited to one module or boundary.
Tests or snapshots can prove no regression.
avoidWhen: The refactor is mixed with feature work.
There is no behavior-preserving verification path.
loops.defaultObjective: Improve the selected code structure without changing behavior.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the narrowest tests or build checks covering the refactored boundary.
stopConditions: Behavior-preserving checks pass.
The refactor starts expanding beyond the selected boundary.
Verification is insufficient to prove safety.
questions: Which module or boundary should be improved?
What behavior must not change?
Which checks prove safety?
loops.body: Define the behavior-preserving boundary, make one structural improvement, rerun focused checks, and stop before the refactor expands into feature work.
goodExamples: clean up one boundary without feature changes
exampleChecks: Run focused tests for the boundary
Run type-check or build for touched files

commonMisroutes.reason: Skill instruction changes need routing and scenario checks.
Performance work needs baseline measurement and proof.

reliability.summary: Reliable as a starter when the refactor boundary is explicit and behavior must not change.
bestWhen: One code boundary or module is selected.
Behavior-preserving checks exist.
The goal is clarity, duplication reduction, or contained structure improvement.
failsWhen: The refactor starts expanding into feature work.
Tests do not cover the selected boundary.
The desired architecture is still undecided.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the narrowest tests or build checks covering the refactored boundary.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to clean up code while protecting behavior from accidental change.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when the goal is internal structure and the current behavior should stay the same.
userGuide.starterRequest: Refactor the checkout module without changing behavior and rerun the existing checks.
userGuide.firstStep: Name the module, current behavior to preserve, and the tests or manual checks that protect it.
userGuide.proofTip: Run the before/after checks and keep unrelated behavior or API changes out of the diff.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it when the real goal is a feature change, a bug fix, or a dependency upgrade.

loops.title: Product evaluation
loops.summary: Run realistic scenarios, repair misses, and prove the flow meets a quality bar.
bestFor: A feature works but quality is uncertain.
Realistic scenarios can be defined.
Pass/fail criteria can be written before fixes.
avoidWhen: The product goal is still unknown.
The scenario needs unavailable users or production data.
loops.defaultObjective: Prove the core user flow across realistic scenarios.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the defined scenarios and record pass/fail evidence.
stopConditions: All scenarios pass.
The quality bar needs human judgment.
Repeated failures indicate the product needs redesign.
questions: Which user flow should be evaluated?
What scenarios represent real use?
What pass/fail criteria matter?
loops.body: Define realistic scenarios and criteria, run them consistently, fix underlying causes of failures, rerun affected scenarios, and periodically rerun the full set.
goodExamples: prove checkout flow meets acceptance criteria
exampleChecks: Rerun affected and full scenario sets after fixes

commonMisroutes.reason: One-screen clarity work is UX polish unless scenario coverage is the goal.
A red check should be repaired before broader product evaluation.

reliability.summary: Useful for structured evaluation, but reliability depends on scenario quality and human judgment.
bestWhen: Realistic scenarios and pass/fail criteria can be written.
The core user flow can be exercised consistently.
Failures can be tied back to fixable causes.
failsWhen: Success criteria are vague or purely taste-based.
The user population or core job is not defined.
Evaluation data cannot be captured consistently.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run the defined scenarios consistently and record pass/fail evidence with fixes for concrete failures.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to judge whether an AI/product behavior works across realistic scenarios.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when quality depends on examples, criteria, and repeatable scenario checks.
userGuide.starterRequest: Evaluate Loop It recommendation quality across realistic user goals and fix clear misses.
userGuide.firstStep: Define the scenarios, expected outcomes, acceptance criteria, and full-check command.
userGuide.proofTip: Rerun affected scenarios after each fix, then run the full evaluation set before stopping.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it for a single bug, styling issue, or implementation task with a normal unit test.

loops.title: Skill instruction hardening
loops.summary: Improve an Agent Skill, prompt, or loop library so routing, examples, and verifier behavior are clearer.
bestFor: A skill or prompt exists but agents misroute or miss important constraints.
The loop library needs examples, scenario coverage, or clearer selection rules.
The change can be verified with selector tests, smoke checks, or prompt-quality scenarios.
avoidWhen: The request is only page copy or visual polish.
The skill behavior cannot be checked with examples or usage scenarios.
loops.defaultObjective: Improve the selected skill instructions and library routing without adding unnecessary scope.
loops.defaultCheck: Run selector scenario checks, package smoke checks, and a focused manual prompt-quality review.
stopConditions: The target scenarios route to the intended loop or instruction path.
The skill metadata, examples, and tests agree.
Further improvement needs real user feedback or host-specific behavior not available locally.
questions: Which skill behavior or route is currently wrong?
What example prompt should succeed after the change?
What test or manual scenario proves the skill improved?
loops.body: Identify the misroute or unclear instruction, add concrete examples and routing signals, update the selector or skill text, run scenario checks, and keep only changes that improve observable behavior.
goodExamples: improve Loop It skill routing and examples
add library examples so recommendations are testable
exampleChecks: Manually inspect generated prompt quality for target examples

commonMisroutes.reason: Code-only cleanup should use refactor containment.
One-screen clarity belongs to UX polish unless skill routing is changing.

reliability.summary: Useful for improving routing and instructions, but needs scenario evals before it can be called proven.
bestWhen: A misroute, unclear instruction, or weak example is known.
Selector scenarios can prove the intended routing behavior.
The scope is the skill text, metadata, library, or verifier behavior.
failsWhen: The desired agent behavior is not specified with examples.
The fix adds broad prompt surface without new evaluation coverage.
No scenario checks exist to catch regressions.
reliability.requiredCheck: Run selector scenario checks, package smoke checks, and a focused manual prompt-quality review.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this to improve a skill so agents choose better actions and avoid bad routes.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when instructions, examples, routing, or guardrails are causing wrong agent behavior.
userGuide.starterRequest: Improve Loop It skill routing and examples, then prove the selector scenarios still pass.
userGuide.firstStep: Describe the misroute or weak behavior, then add an example or scenario that should catch it.
userGuide.proofTip: Run selector scenario checks and smoke tests so the instruction change is not just vibes.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it for general prompt polishing that has no observable routing or behavior check.

loops.title: Codebase intake to running loop
loops.summary: Inspect a repository request, choose the highest-confidence loop, and run one bounded verifier-gated loop.
bestFor: The user wants Loop It to decide what loop to run from a broad repo request.
The repository needs inspection before the right verifier or loop is obvious.
The goal is to stop back-and-forth by turning vague work into one bounded loop.
avoidWhen: The user already provided a specific failing command and failure output.
The user asks for a known loop directly.
The request would require unsafe, production, credential, billing, or external-message actions.
loops.defaultObjective: Inspect the repository, recommend the highest-confidence Loop It loop, and run one bounded pass with proof.
loops.defaultCheck: Run the inferred verifier or ask for one if no safe project-specific check can be found.
stopConditions: A specific loop has been selected and run to a verifier result.
No safe verifier can be inferred and one targeted question has been asked.
The next action requires approval, secrets, production access, or destructive operations.
questions: What user-visible outcome should improve?
What command or manual check should reject bad work?
Should Loop It pick the highest-confidence issue or focus on a known area?
loops.body: Inspect the repository metadata, active .loop-it state, package scripts, CI files, README, and the user request; choose the highest-confidence bundled loop; name the verifier; run one bounded loop in Run The Loop mode; update evidence; stop with proof, a blocker, or one targeted question.
requiredSignals: needs inferred verifier or targeted question
goodExamples: inspect this repo and run the right loop
find the next actionable issue in this codebase
what should Loop It tackle next
improve this repo with the highest-confidence loop
exampleChecks: Infer and run npm run check, npm test, lint, typecheck, or build when available
Record the selected loop id and verifier in .loop-it/progress.json
Stop with one targeted verifier question when no safe check can be inferred

commonMisroutes.reason: Known red checks should go straight to failing CI repair.
Known release work should use release readiness.
Known security findings should use security hardening.

reliability.summary: Useful as a repo-intake router, but it needs more real-project evals before being called proven.
bestWhen: The request is broad but the repo has scripts, CI, docs, or active loop state to inspect.
The user wants Loop It to reduce back-and-forth by picking one actionable loop.
A safe verifier can be inferred or one targeted question can unblock it.
failsWhen: The repo has no trustworthy verifier and the user will not clarify success.
The request hides multiple unrelated goals.
The highest-confidence next step requires approval or production access.
reliability.requiredCheck: Record the selected loop, inferred verifier, first verifier result, changed files, blockers, and next action.

userGuide.plainLanguage: Use this when you want Loop It to inspect the repo, pick the right loop, and start running it.
userGuide.useWhen: Pick this when the user asks broadly what to fix, improve, or tackle next in a codebase.
userGuide.starterRequest: Inspect this repo, recommend the highest-confidence loop, and run it with proof.
userGuide.firstStep: Read package scripts, CI config, README, active loop state, and the user request before choosing a loop.
userGuide.proofTip: The result must name the selected loop and verifier, then show a real verifier result or a blocker.
userGuide.notFor: Do not use it when the user already named a specific failing command, release task, security finding, or doc mismatch.
